coredata.dc:description: Sensing of amino acid serves as the frontier research area for early diagnosis and monitoring various diseases. Among various amino acids, the sensing of L-Cysteine is much important for detection of human diseases like neurotoxic effect and coronary heart disease which arises due to excess of L-Cysteine. To address this, we propose a very simple method of L-Cys sensing via fluorescence “TURN ON” mechanism involving silver centred Rhodamine B nanogranules (AgNPs/RhB) stabilized via electrostatic interaction. The as-synthesized nanocomposite fluorescence probe shows highly selective sensing towards L-Cysteine aided by the preferential formation of stable covalent linkage between AgNPs and thiol group of L-Cys which is supported by FTIR and XPS study. The superior selectivity of L-Cysteine in presence of other amino acids and interactive ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.084 µM and working linear range of 100–2200 µM makes the study a useful addition to the existing literature. The responsiveness of nanogranules to extreme conditions of ionic strength and pH further establishes its stability and suitability for present application. Moreover, the excellent recovery percentages obtained in real human serum samples establishes its effectiveness in diagnostic fields.

head.ce:title: Rhodamine B embedded silver nanogranules for selective sensing of l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018 Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensing of amino acid serves as the frontier research area for early diagnosis and monitoring various diseases. Among various amino acids, the sensing of L-Cysteine is much important for detection of human diseases like neurotoxic effect and coronary heart disease which arises due to excess of L-Cysteine. To address this, we propose a very simple method of L-Cys sensing via fluorescence “TURN ON” mechanism involving silver centred Rhodamine B nanogranules (AgNPs/RhB) stabilized via electrostatic interaction. The as-synthesized nanocomposite fluorescence probe shows highly selective sensing towards L-Cysteine aided by the preferential formation of stable covalent linkage between AgNPs and thiol group of L-Cys which is supported by FTIR and XPS study. The superior selectivity of L-Cysteine in presence of other amino acids and interactive ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.084 µM and working linear range of 100–2200 µM makes the study a useful addition to the existing literature. The responsiveness of nanogranules to extreme conditions of ionic strength and pH further establishes its stability and suitability for present application. Moreover, the excellent recovery percentages obtained in real human serum samples establishes its effectiveness in diagnostic fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: L-Cysteine (L-Cys) is a sulfur-containing amino acid, found in many peptides, proteins, and glutathione. It is responsible for breakdown of phospholipids and the reduction of cells, which leads to the restoration of liver function [1–3]. Furthermore, its derivatives can be applied to treat the harmful side effects caused by antimony, heavy metals, radioactive drugs, and other substances [4]. L-Cys is one of the most significant biomarkers for early diagnosis and treatment along with monitoring the progression of diseases [5]. From various studies it was observed that abnormal levels of L-Cys have been linked to several human diseases, including neurotoxic effect, growth retardation, coronary heart disease and Alzheimer's disease [6,7]. Therefore, detection of L-Cys in human serum samples is of great significance. So far, researchers have approached different methods for detection of L-Cys which includes colorimetric [8], electrochemical [9], fluorescence [10,11] and photo-electrochemical methods [12,13]. Among these methods, fluorescence method has shown better results for routine investigation. In a recent study, Au-Ag nanoclusters, Au nanorods, and Ag nanoparticles were considered for L-Cys detection using fluorescence method by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [14]. Similarly, carbon quantum dots and N-acetyl-l-cysteine capped AuNPs were used for L-Cys detection based on FRET effect [15]. In another study, carbon dots were taken for fluorescence detection of L-Cys molecules [16].

Rhodamine B (RhB) belongs to xanthene family which is a fluorescent reagent with both fluorophore and chromophore properties. The photophysical properties, including a large molar extinction coefficient, long emission wavelength, low background interference, and high fluorescence quantum yield of RhB could be very useful for designing ion probes [17–19]. In a recent investigation, RhB was used as a chemosensor for L-Cys detection over other amino acids [20]. Similarly, a fluorescence nanoprobe was developed using carbon dots and RhB for selective detection of L-Cysteine [21]. Such FRET sensors have been used as fluorescence “OFF-ON” probes in detection of a wide range of chemicals and bio-analytes [22–25]. The present study focuses on the detection of L-Cys using fluorometric assays containing silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), thanks to their level free and convenient detection process in aqueous medium, even at considerable low concentrations. The silver-sulfur bond shows strong interaction which immobilizes the sulfur containing groups on the surface of the silver nanoparticles, enabling it for feasible recognition of sulfur containing compounds [26–28]. AgNPs are the most readily prepared, adaptable, and affordable nanomaterial of all the metal composites that have been reported to far [26,27]. AgNPs have shown a drastic shift in their photonic characteristics through aggregation mechanism. They also exhibit exceptionally high extinction coefficients and their ability to modify their plasmonic band in response to size and distance, makes them a potential candidate as a sensor for a range of various target analytes such as drugs [29], toxic metal ions [30] and biomolecules [31] in pharmaceutical and biological samples [32].

Althoughfew papersare availableforthedetectionofdifferentaminoacids using RhB, however the formation ofAgNPs/RhBnanogranulesforL-Cyssensingisnot reported. Further, self-assembly of RhB molecules around the AgNPs through electrostatic interaction and formation of covalent Ag-S bond from the thiol group of L-Cys is the primary focus of the present work. The sensing mechanism solely relies on the competitive binding stabilities between RhB, AgNPs and L-Cys, which subsequently detects L-Cys via fluorescence “Turn ON” technique. The electrostatic interaction between AgNPs and RhB depends upon the stability of trisodium citrate reduced silver nanoparticles. The optimization of reaction conditions for proper capping of trisodium citrate on AgNPs is a crucial challenge to control the size and stability of silver nanogranules which is necessary to establish the electrostatic interaction. AgNPs/RhB nanogranules are prepared using simple, green technique and its sensitivity, selectivity and interference are studied in different stimuli responsive parameters to investigate its suitability and stability for practical use. The synthesized nanocomposite focuses on providing lower limit of detection values along with high selectivity and linear response towards L-Cys. The superior sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed sensing system has been established in terms of better limit of detection and linear range in comparison with previous reports. The work is further extended for successful detection of L-Cys in human blood serum samples to establish its effectiveness in diagnostic applications.

The amino acid L-Cys (C3H7NO2, M.W: 175.63 g/mol), as well as 17 other essential and non-essential amino acids including as L-Glycine, L-Lycine, DL-Methionine, L-Arginine, DL-Valine, L-Proline, L-Histidine, DL-Aspartic acid, L-Tyrosine, L-Ornithine, DL-Serine, L-Alanine, L-Hydroxyproline, DL-Homocysteine, L-Leucine, DL-Glutamic acid, and DL-Tryptophan are utilized in this study, were procured from Central Drug House (P) Ltd., headquartered at 7/28 Vardaan House, Daryaganj, New Delhi-110002, India. Rhodamine B (RhB) (C28H31ClN2O3, M.W: 479.02 g/mol) was also acquired from CDH. Silver nitrate (AgNO3, M.W: 169.87 g/mol) was sourced from Rankem chemicals. Prior to experimentation, the purity of RhB was verified using UV–Vis and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic methods [33]. Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7, M.W: 294.10 g/mol) was produced from Central Drug House (P) Ltd., headquartered at 7/28 Vardaan House, Daryaganj, New Delhi-110002, India, Potassium chloride (KCl, M.W: 74.5513 g/mol), Sodium chloride (NaCl, M.W: 58.45 g/mol) were procured from Central Drug House, New Delhi. The aqueous concentrations of the chemicals were made using double deionized water.

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were synthesized using the citrate reduction technique as per previously reported method [34]. A 50 ml aqueous solution containing 1.5 mM AgNO3 was heated at 80 °C, with continuous stirring for 30 min. Then, 6.2 ml of trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) (1 %) was gradually added to the solution, dropwise, maintaining a constant temperature. The solution was subjected to stirring for 20 min till the colour changes to yellow. The appearance the brownish yellow colour, which occurs due to typical surface plasmon resonance effect, indicates the formation of AgNPs.

A 5 mL portion of AgNPs solution was stirred continuously at room temperature (25 °C) for 5 min alongside a 1 mL amount of a 1 × 10−5 μM RhB solution, which acted as the fluorescent probe molecule, initiating the formation of the assay. Subsequently, freshly prepared L-Cys solutions at various concentrations (ranging from 1 × 10−1 µM to 2 × 103 µM) were individually introduced (0.5 mL each) into the assay and allowed to incubate for 10 min. Following this incubation period, fluorescence spectra were recorded. AgNPs/RhB solution was combined with separately prepared concentrations of L-Cys and various amino acids to validate the selectivity of assay. Each mixed solution was incubated for 10 min before fluorescence emission was measured. The procedure was repeated three time for consistency of results to ensure repeatability. Scheme 1 a shows the overall preparatory process schematically.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the materials were analysed in 200–800 nm range using a double beam UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu’s UV-1900, Shimadzu, Japan) preceded by proper baseline correction for the solvent background. The steady state fluorescence spectra of the materials were captured in 545–700 nm wavelength range using an Agilent-Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer (Agilent Technologies., USA). To measure the steady state fluorescence emission, an excitation light source of 260 nm wavelength emerging from a slit with a 2 nm width was used. At room temperature (25 °C), the emission and absorption spectra were recorded from the aqueous solution using a quartz cell having a path length of 1.0 cm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys nanocomposite was recorded via PHI-5000 Versa Probe-II (Ulvac-PHI Inc.). The surface morphology of the nanocomposite was analysed by GeminiSEM 500 Field emission surface electron microscope (FESEM) by using PVA as a template to cast films. High Resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) investigation of AgNPs and AgNPs/RhB was recorded on a high-Resolution transmission electron microscope functioned at 200 keV (FEI Technai T20, FEI Company, USA). For HRTEM analysis, the aqueous solutions were kept for 15 min incubation followed by addition of small drop which was spread properly onto the carbon coated copper microgrid and dried at room temperature afterward. The FTIR spectrum for AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys was acquired within 4000 to 400 cm−1 frequency range using a Perkin-Elmer FTIR spectrometer, USA. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out employing the Zetasizer Nano Instrument with the model number ZEN-3690, manufactured by Malvern.

The suggested technique uses AgNPs/RhB nanogranules to detect L-Cys by analysing fluorescence emission spectra. First, under predetermined reaction conditions, desired concentrations of RhB solution and AgNPs solution are combined and taken for fluorescence study. There was a decrease in fluorescence intensity i.e., “Turn OFF” was observed. Subsequently, after adding L-Cys to the reaction solution, there was a sudden increase in fluorescence intensity to “Turn ON” state, showing signals the successful sensing of L-Cys (Scheme 1b).

Here, we methodically developed a highly responsive detection system used for the accurate and specific identification of L-Cys. Our approach relies on the fluorometric assessment of AgNPs/RhB within an aqueous environment for the detection process. The schematic representation of the mechanism behind L-Cys detection is provided in the Scheme 1b. Upon investigation, it was noted that the aqueous solution containing AgNPs/RhB exhibited minimal emission intensity due to effective FRET transfer arising due to excited RhB molecules to the surface of AgNPs. This phenomenon was attributed to both the efficient FRET process and the reduction in the radiative rate constant of each dye molecule that is attached to the NP surface. The transfer of energy from the donor RhB to the acceptor AgNPs primarily occurred through the FRET process [35]. However, something interesting occurred when L-Cys was mixed to the AgNPs/RhB solution. the fluorescence emission peak became much more intense from its previously quenched state. This observation strongly suggested that the RhB molecules, initially got adsorbed on to the AgNPs surface, experienced significant recovery, probably because of a strong interaction that the thiol group (−SH) facilitates between L-Cys and AgNPs. The strong binding of L-Cys molecules to AgNPs caused by the thiol group, that hashigh affinity for AgNPs caused RhB molecules to be detached from the surfaceof the NPs. RhB molecules adhered to AgNPs through electrostatic interaction, whereas AgNPs preferred L-Cys via Ag–S bonds through chemisorption, which is considerably stronger.

The selectivity of the as prepared fluorometric assay for L-Cys detection by using AgNPs/RhB is shown by investigating the fluorescence emission spectra of the nangranules with various amino acids including L-Glycine, L-Lysine, DL-Methionine, L-Arginine, DL-Valine, L-Proline, L-Histidine, DL-Aspartic acid, L-Tyrosine, L-Ornithine, DL-Serine, L-Alanine, L-Hydroxyproline, DL-Homocysteine, L-Leucine, DL-Glutamic acid, and DL-Tryptophan. It is noteworthy to mention that the concentrations of all amino acid solutions are kept identical to that of the L-Cys solution during the fluorescence observations.

Fresh human blood serum was collected from the RDC of Veer Surendra Sai Institute of Medical Science and Research (VSSIMSAR), Burla, Sambalpur, India. The samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min followed by filtration using Whatman filter paper to remove suspended particles, if any. The filtrate was diluted 10-folds using deionized water and spiked with different concentrations of L-Cys, i.e., 10. 20. 30, 40 and 50 μM. The spiked samples were studied for fluorescence recoveries and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were calculated. The entire procedure was in accordance with the guidelines of the Ethical Committee of VSSIMSAR, with proper consent from the participants preceding the experiments.

The AgNPs/RhB is prepared by simple and convenient solution blending technique with an objective to establish a direct linkage between the fluorescent probe and the surface of AgNPs via electrostatic interactions which provide good solubility in aqueous medium and photostability. This stability is due to the RhB molecules acting as stabilizing agent to the citrate reduced AgNPs up to 4–5 days in dark at a temperature condition of 4 ℃. The targeted fluorescence recovery is achieved by the formation of stronger Ag—S bond with the interaction with L-Cys, forming a much stable system and release of RhB molecules from surface of AgNPs.

The surface morphology of the AgNPs/RhB is revealed in Fig. 1 a–c. The clustering effect of AgNPs to form nanogranules of various geometrical shapes is clearly perceived. Similar biosynthesized Ag aggregates have been reported in earlier literatures [36]. Further, the micrographs show the formation of well-dispersed and interconnected pores, facilitated by the self-assembly of the nanoparticles which provides active sites for adsorption of L-Cys to facilitate the reaction with AgNPs. Fig. 1d shows the EDS data of the synthesized AgNPs/RhB which essentially confirms the presence of AgNPs in the nanocomposite system. The table present in the inset of Fig. 1d shows the weight and atomic percentages of respective elements found on the surface. The dots observed in Fig. 1f-i represents the respective elements found on the cross-sectional area illustrated in Fig. 1e. The spatial dispersion of Ag atoms supports the clustering effect of AgNPs to form the nanogranules having average size of 300 nm.

The HRTEM micrographs of the spherical shaped silver nanoparticles are visible in the Fig. 2 a and magnified images in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c. Subspherical shaped AgNPs are observed which are aggregated and self-assembled with formation of nanogranules. In the HRTEM image of AgNPs generated through biosynthesis using secondary metabolites derived from Bacillus subtilis (SDUM301120), a comparable observation is evident [37]. The TEM image in Fig. 2d and magnified image in Fig. 2f are represented for AgNPs/RhB nanogranules. Average particle size measured versus count% is represented in Fig. 2e, where the average particles size is found to be 43.01. Metallic nanogranules prove to be a viable class of fluorescent amino acid sensors as compared to metallic nanoparticles because the nanogranular surface morphology accentuates the surface area to volume ratio by more than 50 % and thus improving the analyte capturing efficiency and probe saturation [38]. Hence, the formation of AgNPs/RhB nanogranules may provide a better option for our proposed mechanism for L-Cys detection. The HRTEM image of the AgNPs/RhB nanogranules reveals lattice fringes at 2 nm and 5 nm magnifications which substantiates the crystalline characteristics of the produced AgNPs. The width of these in-plane lattice fringes is measured to be 0.16 nm and 0.24 nm in Fig. 2g and Fig. 2h. The lattice fringes in the HRTEM image indicating a spacing of 0.16 nm (Fig. 2g), corresponds to the (220) planes of silver [39]. The inter-fringe distance measured at 0.24 nm (Fig. 2h), aligns with the (111) plane of face-centered cubic (fcc) silver crystals, as per the Joint Committee of Powder Diffraction Standard (JCPDS) Card No-04-0783 [40]. Insets in these figures show the fast Fourier transform (FFT) images of the focused areas. Also, the grain size is measured from the selected area electron diffraction pattern of AgNPs/RhB nanogranules shown in Fig. 2i. Additionally, the circular rings in the image served as evidence of the crystalline nature of the Ag NPs/RhB.

The as-prepared AgNPs in aqueous colloidal solution exhibit a unique absorption band revealing the distinctive silver surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak at 409 nm and a faint hump at 271 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum, which signifies the formation of AgNPs as depicted in Fig. 3 a [41,42]. The UV–Vis peaks of pure RhB and AgNPs/RhB is given in Fig. 3a. The pure RhB displays a prominent absorption band at approximately 562 nm, associated with the π-π* transition of the RhB molecules. Peaks at similar wavelength is reported for RhB in previous literatures [43]. At low concentrations in an aqueous solution, RhB dimers and monomers are in equilibrium, and at a particular concentration level, their average number should remain constant [45]. Nevertheless, in our study, the RhB solution concentration is approximately 1x10−5 μM, specifically chosen to detect of L-Cys. Because of their incredibly low concentration, it is presumed that the majority of RhB molecules remained in the examined solutionas monomer units. In the UV–Vis spectrum of the AgNPs/RhB solution, the typical peak of AgNPs is observed at 422 nm, while the primary characteristic absorption band of RhB remained nearly unchanged at 562 nm. However, its intensity is reduced. The alteration in the absorption spectrum of dye molecules in the presence of AgNPs could be attributed to their adsorption onto the surface of the nanoparticles. The incorporation of AgNPs into the RhB aqueous solution indeed induced changes in the microenvironment surrounding of the electronic states of the RhB molecules. It could potentially lead to modifications in electric transition dipole moment of the dye molecules. The interaction between the molecular dipoles of thedye moleculesand the plasmonic field generated by AgNPs eventually influenced the excitation energy ofeach dye molecule, which reduces the absorbance of the RhB monomeric band at 562 nm [43]. After addition of L-Cys, a new SPR peak at 528 nm is observed. The SPR peaks of AgNPs and RhB is sufficiently reduced as compared to prior addition of L-Cys, indicating the aggregation of the nanoparticles [44].

The interaction of the AgNPs, RhB and L-Cys are characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b and c). In Fig. 3b, presence of –SH group is confirmed by the presence of stretching peak at 2549 cm−1. The RhB spectrum exhibits >CO and CN stretching peaks at 1689 cm−1 and 1327 cm−1, respectively. The AgNPs/RhB spectrum shows peaks at 1614 cm−1, 1287 cm−1 and 1083 cm−1 corresponding to >CO stretching of carboxylic group, C–N stretching and C–O stretching of RhB, respectively. The appearance of >CO peak at lower wavenumber may indicate the existence of electrostatic interactions between AgNPs and RhB [46]. In the FTIR spectra of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys, broad peak at 3326 cm−1 represents –OH stretching of carboxyl group of L-Cys. The peaks at 1638 cm−1, 1259 cm−1, and 1090 cm−1 are assigned to carboxylic > C = O stretching, C–N stretching and C–O stretching of cysteine, respectively. Another interesting observation is the absence of –SH peak of L-Cys at 2500 cm−1 [47], which provides major evidence regarding the engagement of –SH group in formation of covalent bond with AgNPs. Similar results are reported for Cysteine capped gold nanoparticles for sensing of trinitrotoluene [48]. Furthermore, the additional peak appearing at 523 cm−1 in AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys represents Ag–S stretching frequency, which confirms the formation of covalent linkage between L-Cys and AgNPs [49].

The electronic structure of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys is studied by X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 4 (a–f)). The survey scan reveals the presence of C, O, N, S, and Ag elements at 285.2 eV, 496.0 eV, 396.0 eV, 165.1 eV and 531.0 eV respectively in the sample (Fig. 4(a)). The high resolution deconvoluted spectral data for C1s at 283.0 eV, 284.9 eV, and 287.2 eV corresponds to C–C/CC bond, C-N/C-S bond, and CO bond respectively (Fig. 4(b)) [50]. In Fig. 4(c), the deconvoluted peaks for O1s at 529.8 eV, 531.0 eV and 534.3 eV are attributed to CO, C–O–C/COH, and COOH bonds, respectively. The peaks for N 1 s at 398.3 eV and 399.7 eV represent O–N and OC–N bonds, respectively. High resolution scan of Ag 3d and S 2p spectra are shown in Fig. 4e, f respectively. The deconvolution spectra of Ag 3d is fitted with two silver doublets. The binding energy at 366.7 eV corresponds to metallic Ag0, while the second peak at 372.8 eV corresponds to Ag-S showing the bond formation between nanoparticle and cysteine [51,52]. S 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 4e. The deconvoluted peaks revealed presence of three chemical states of sulfur at 160.7 eV, 162.3 eV and 163.5 eV corresponding to Ag-S bond, S-H bond, and CH2-S bond respectively. The above spectral data provides strong evidence that L-Cys molecules are covalently bonded to the surface of the nano silver [53].

The size distribution of AgNPs/RhB and AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys in colloidal suspension is illustrated using the DLS technique in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively. The AgNPs/RhB particle size is 340.7 nm, and its low polydispersity index (PDI) is 0.688, indicating monodispersed particles. The lesser size distribution of AgNPs/RhB is attributed to the electrostatic repulsion between RhB and AgNPs/RhB [54]. Upon adding L-Cys, the size increases to 649.5 nm having PDI of 0.776, suggesting a broader size distribution of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys [55]. The increasing value of PDI value evidences the detachment of RhB dye molecules from the nanogranules to be replaced by L-Cys molecules on the surface of AgNPs, which coincides with the results obtained in FESEM analysis. This increase in particle size is attributed to L-Cys, which acts as a capping agent, enhancing particle stability. The Ag-S bond formation with L-Cys further contributes to stabilization of the AgNPs. Previous literatures also used L-Cys as a capping agent for AgNPs [56] and AuNPs [57]. On the other hand, RhB binds to AgNPs through electrostatic adsorption. Although, it enhances the stability of the nanoparticle suspension however, RhB forms a comparatively lesser stable system with AgNPs than L-Cys, which can establish strong Ag—S bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of L-cysteine by AgNPs/RhB fluorescence assay

ce:section.ce:para: Using control experiments, the rationale behind the choice of RhB modified AgNPs nanocomposites is explained (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence emission spectra of RhB at different excitation wavelength (220–320 nm) is studied (Fig. 6c). It is found that at 260 nm excitation wavelength, the RhB solution produces highest fluorescence intensity. RhB exhibits the fluorescence emission peak at 575 nm. Remarkably, the introduction of L-Cys to RhB in the absence of AgNPs results in minimal alteration in fluorescence emission spectra compared to RhB. Instead, there is a notable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of RhB@L-Cys. After adding L-Cys solutions at varying concentrations, a slight decrease in RhB emission intensity is observed, which is mainly caused by diluting RhB concentration in the mixed solution, rather than any interaction in their excited or ground states. This phenomenon underscores the significance of AgNPs in influencing the fluorescence behavior. The diminished fluorescence intensity of RhB@L-Cys, without AgNPs, implies that the synergistic effect of AgNPs with both RhB and L-Cys is crucial for establishing fluorescence properties due to the quenching characteristics of metal nanoparticles. Thus, employing RhB alone is inadequate for sensing studies. The fluorescence emission spectra of AgNPs/RhB and AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys is also examined in Fig. 6a. Upon incorporation of AgNPs into RhB, the emission intensity of RhB at 575 nm decreases by 75 %, indicating the quenching effects by AgNPs. Again, subsequent interaction with L-Cys leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity by 60 %, emphasizing the “Turn ON” mechanism for L-Cys detection. This observation strongly implies that the RhB molecules, initially got adsorbed to AgNPs surface via electrostatic interactions prior to the introduction of L-Cys, experienced notable recovery.

In the study, fluorescence analysis of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys is conducted at various RhB concentrations ranging from 1-6 µM, with the concentrations of AgNPs and L-Cys held constant at 1.5 mM and 1000 µM respectively (Fig. 6b). The results show that as the concentration of RhB is increased, the intensity of the fluorescence emission also increases. This concludes that the increase in fluorescence intensity is due to increase in concentration of RhB since the interaction between AgNPs and L-Cys is stronger than the interaction between AgNPs and RhB. Even if the concentration of RhB is increased, the fluorescence intensity did not decrease. This observation can be explained by the fluorescent properties of RhB.

The fluorescence quenching phenomena of the fluorophores due to the presence of AgNPs can be suitably designated to FRET transfer from the RhB molecules in excited state onto the AgNPs surface, known as fluorescence “TURN OFF” [58] This also leads to the lowering of radiative rate constant of distinct RhB molecules associated to the surface of AgNPs. The feasibility of FRET is evidently perceived from the overlapping area between the emission spectrum of RhB at 575 nm and the absorption spectrum of AgNPs at 409 nm (Fig. 7 a) [59]. Furthermore, the FRET efficiency (ФET) is calculated using the formula ФET = 1− (I/I0), where I and I0 denote the fluorescence emission intensities of AgNPs/RhB and RhB, respectively, and is found to be 75.65 % [60]. The minimalistic fluorescence emission intensity of AgNPs/RhB implies the adsorption of RhB molecules on the negative charge rich surface of NPs via electrostatic forces, as interpreted in Fig. 7b. Upon incubation with L-Cys, a more favourable adsorption of L-Cys as compared to RhB takes place. The AgNPs establishes highly stable covalent bonds with the thiol group of Cys which releases RhB, thus disabling the FRET mechanism. This results in recovery of fluorescence intensity making the system, fluorescence “TURN ON”.

It is crucial to explore the stimuli responsive characteristics of a sensing system to prevent issues such as instability and inaccuracies in the outcomes, given that numerous variables, including ionic strength and pH, influence the sensing process in complicated biological enviroments. Therefore, we have investigated the sensing behaviour of nanocomposite under the condition of different pH and varying ionic strength levels. The effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescent properties of AgNPs/RhB and AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys is studied after the introduction of NaCl solutions at varying concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 1 M, as depicted in Fig. 8 a. There are no significant alterations in the fluorescence intensity observed regardless of the concentration of NaCl. It suggests that the fluorescent properties of AgNPs/RhB system are not impacted by the presence of the ions and the AgNPs/RhB sensing system possess advantageous properties for suitable use in environments where high salt concentrations are present. The fluorescent properties of AgNPs/RhB and AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys nanogranules in response to varying pH conditions are investigated, with pH levels ranging from 2 to 13, as depicted in Fig. 8b. From the results, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite remained consistent across all acidic pH levels. However, under basic pH conditions, the fluorescence intensity of AgNPs/RhB@L-Cys gradually increases (Fig. 8b (inset)). This can be attributed to the deprotonation of thiol and carboxylic acid groups in L-cysteine at high alkaline pH levels. The deprotonation of these groups increases the number of free thiols in the solution, which can more readily complex the nanoparticle surface [61].

Exploring the capacity of a fluorescentassayfor selective sensing is essential, especially in the complex biological environment where a variety of interfering species could affect the sensing process directly or indirectly and result in inconsistent measurements. Consequently, we have investigated the selectivity of the AgNPs/RhB nanogranules for the specific detection of L-Cys in the presence of various amino acid molecules, such as L-Glycine, L-Lycine, DL-Methionine, DL-Valine, L-Arginine, L-Proline, L-Histidine, DL-Aspartic acid, L-Tyrosine, L-Ornithine, DL-Serine, L-Alanine, L-Hydroxyproline, DL-Homocysteine, L-Leucine, DL-Glutamic acid, and DL-Tryptophan. Fluorescence measurements are performed on equivalent concentrations of all other amino acids, including L-Cys, after adding them into the reaction system.

Notably, when L-Cys is present, there is a substantial rise in fluorescence intensity compared to other amino acids, as depicted in the Fig. 9 a. This identification clearly highlights the ability of AgNPs/RhB sensing system in identifying and locating L-Cys among other amino acid molecules. However, among all the amino acids taken for study, three vital amino acids i.e., L-Histidine, DL-Methionine, and L-Homocysteine exhibited marginally better recovery rates. In case of L-Histidine the significant fluorescence enhancement may result from the electrostatic interaction of –NH2 with AgNPs. It is important to note that, in comparison to L-Cys, AgNPs are not likely to self-assemble with DL-Methionine and L-Homocysteine amino acids through covalent bonding using S–Agbonds within the similar aqueous medium. The thiol moiety (−SH) of L-Cys functions as its head group that remains further prominently unprotected to the surrounding aqueous environment, which increases the extent of interaction with AgNPs. Also, DL-methionine contains sulphur group but lacks thiol group, which reduces its affinity for AgNPs. When equivalent molar concentrations of both amino acids are considered during the assay experiments, L-Homocysteine, a homologue of L-Cys, has roughly a third less fluorescence recovery efficiency than L-Cys due to its extra methylene bridge. The mechanistic representation of the interaction of different amino acids with AgNPs is given in Fig. 9b. Additionally, we have successfully verified the selectivity of our suggested method for detecting L-Cys in the presence of cations such as Na+ and K+. The distinct and precise attribute of this sensing probe highlights the consistency and precision of the methodology for analysing L-Cys in real samples.

The sensing probe is subjected to anti-interference experiments to verify its specificity towards L-Cys in the presence of other amino acids and coexisting metal ions since the biological fluids contain all the essential and non-essential amino acids (Fig. 9c). From the investigation, it is apparent that the emission spectra increases when the amino acid samples are mixed with L-Cys, due to the presence of free –SH group that can form metal-sulphur bond competitively and quickly resulting in displacement of RhB molecules more efficiently as compared to other amino acids. Some of the emission spectra of different amino acid mixtures are higher as compared to other mixtures. This may be due to minimal interaction of these amino acids with metal surface resulting in displacement of more RhB molecules. When RhB molecules are displaced from the metal surface, the FRET transfer between AgNPs and RhB is reduced which in turn increases fluorescence intensity. The high fluorescence intensity of L-Cys mixtures with other amino acid dictates the specificity of AgNPs/RhB sensor for L-Cys.

A comprehensive investigation into the time-dependent fluorescence characteristics is undertaken to assess both the response time and stability of AgNPs/RhB, in the presence and absence of L-Cys. Emission spectra are meticulously recorded at 5-minute intervals over the course of 1 h. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of AgNPs/RhB exhibits remarkable stability throughout the entire duration of analysis. Contrarily, the introduction of L-Cys with a concentration of 1000 μM results in a prompt and substantial enhancement in fluorescence intensity, observable within the initial 5 min. This heightened intensity persists up to approximately 15 min, after which it is stabilized, maintaining a consistent level for an extended period (Fig. 10 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection (LOD) and linear range of L-Cys detection at various concentrations by AgNPs/RhB fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of the AgNPs/RhB solution across a range of L-Cys concentrations from 1 × 10−1 µM to 2 × 103 µM are depicted in Fig. 10b. It is noticeable that there is an eventually rise in emission intensity as the concentration of L-Cys increases. A calibration curve is produced by graphing the intensity values on the y-axis versus the L-Cys concentrations on the x-axis which is illustrated in the Fig. 10c. The calibration plot depicted in a clear straight linear correlationbetween the various emission intensities and L-Cys concentrations is shown in Fig. 10d, as evidenced by the regression equation y = 0.1614x + 513.3 and a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9754. The calibration plot indicates that the suggested L-Cys sensing method has a linearrange of 1 × 102 µM to 2 × 103 µM. Furthermore, by applying the formula LOD = 3σ/m, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation and ‘m’ is the slope of the calibration plot shown in the Fig. 10d, the detection limit is found to be 1.084 μM.

The practical applicability of the as-synthesized AgNPs/RhB nanosensor is determined using spiked human blood serums. The collected serum samples are diluted 10-folds to minimize the matrix effect followed by addition of known concentrations of L-Cys for calculating the spike fluorescence recoveries [62]. The recovery intensities and RSD values (n = 3) of all the serum samples are enlisted in Table 1 . The fluorescence recoveries vary within a range of 96.7–104.2 % with RSD value lying within the range of 0.2 %, which essentially proves the feasibility of AgNPs/RhB nanogrannules as effective nanosensors for L-Cys detection in biological fluids. The samples 2, 3 and 4 report higher L-Cys recovery, indicating the presence of the amino acid in the serums before spiking. The overall assessment of the recovery % may imply high efficiency of the as-synthesized AgNPs/RhB probe in blood serum samples. Hence, the present study reports a simple system with superior L-Cys sensing performance with high effectiveness in ex vivo trials. The estimated LOD and linear range are better compared to those of many previously reported L-Cys sensing systems utilizing nanocomposites. The superior sensing performance of our synthesized AgNPs/RhB nanogranules in comparison to other materials reported in literature is reflected in Table 2 .

RhB associated silver nanocomposites are successfully synthesized by a simple and green solution blending method, stabilized by electrostatic adsorption of RhB on the surface of nanosilver. The AgNPs/RhB sensing system is highly selective towards L-Cys against other amino acids and interactive ions. The LOD is found to be very low as 1.084 µM with sensitivity around a wide linear range of 100–2200 µM. Stability of the sensing system is established by stimuli responsive studies conducted in extreme ionic strength and pH environments. The reliability of the sensor is confirmed by detection of L-Cys in human blood serum samples with a recovery rate of 100 ± 4 %. The high sensitivity, selectivity as well as stability of the proposed AgNPs/RhB sensing platform makes it suitable for real-world diagnostic applications.
